text: Please address the caveats of using OpenAlex for scientometrics, in particular the difficulties related to paper deduplication. It would be beneficial if you could slightly extend your discussion of these issues. For instance, what is an example of a "regional database" that could be used to complement your corpus?

What is the added value of this new dataset? The referee suggests that you could display a citation network, but networks of institutions and/or co-authors are also possibilities to highlight the key actors in this literature. Alternatively, you could point more clearly toward text analysis and content classification to document how the focus of the literature has shifted. I am not asking you to conduct these analyses, but rather to expand the discussion in your conclusion to highlight the research directions and areas that your database enables. Another way would be to make a brief reference to the research questions explored in your companion paper in Œconomia.
On a side note, I would like you to introduce a table describing the variables contained in your main dataset, “climate_finance_corpus.csv”, after you describe it in the main text.

It would also be beneficial to introduce a bit more structure to your Zenodo package. This will make it easier for the reader to distinguish between the raw data inputs (e.g., “bibcnrs_works.csv”) and the final data products related to this paper (e.g., “climate_finance_corpus.csv”, “embeddings.npz”, and “citations.csv”).

The paper will not necessarily go back to the referees. When you revise, please also prepare a summary of the main revisions and a point-by-point reply to the referee report and to me. I would greatly appreciate getting the paper back within three months. Please do email me if you cannot meet this deadline.

This second advance led to a first question. If I am not mistaken, the corpus does not include in the grey literature documents from national institutions: government or parliamentary reports, reports from economic analysis councils, communications from central banks, etc. Why is that? What is the main justification? Has the author considered a way to incorporate them in order to broaden both the coverage and multilingual scope of his corpus?

The six-step pipeline for cleaning the corpus, combined with four protection criteria, appears to do a good job [...]. However, I am unsure to what extent this pipeline is robust enough to address the many challenges posed by the use of OpenAlex when it comes to drawing conclusions about the dynamics of a research field.

Footnote 1: See for instance Campiglio, E., Deyris, J., Romelli, D., & Scalisi, G. (2025). Warning words in a warming world: Central bank communication and climate change. European Economic Review, 105101.

OpenAlex is the subject of hype in scientometrics research that is not entirely unjustified. It has the best coverage in terms of quantity, includes working papers, and is open-source. The quality of the data retrieved from OpenAlex is however subject to caution.

There are numerous inconsistencies in the metadata: incorrect citation assignments, missing abstracts, duplicates, and sometimes missing full-text. Furthermore, queries in OpenAlex generate false positives. Since OpenAlex is the source of 95% of the corpus—both the raw corpus and the refined one—it seems necessary that the author, in the article, provide: first, a clear explanation of the various limitations posed by OpenAlex; then a clear explanation of how his pipeline addresses each of them.

While including working papers is an advantage in terms of coverage scope, it poses a problem of duplicates. The DOI-based deduplication proposed by the author does not always work, as the DOIs (if any) differ between the working paper version and the published version. Similarly, deduplication based on publication date + title may not be sufficient, since the publication date (and, more rarely, the title) differs between a WP and a published article. I believe the deduplication process would benefit from being improved, maybe by incorporating the author variable? Furthermore, could the author specify in the text how many duplicates are removed by each of these two procedures: 1) DOI-based and 2) title+year matching?

OpenAlex contains a huge number of incorrect citation assignments. For example, it attributes citations of a book to the book reviews published in a journal. Above all, the list of papers cited by a document may be erroneous — empty for some articles and completely aberrant for others. Did the author take note of the number of references cited by each document in the corpus? How does the author address this issue? Unless I am mistaken, this is not specified. The idea suggested by the author of performing full-text reference parsing (e.g., via GROBID) to correct citation issues seems necessary, given the importance of citation analysis on the one hand, and OpenAlex’s role in this dataset on the other.
The article would benefit from presenting a network of citations drawn from this database at this stage, in order to demonstrate its potential and/or limitations in this regard.

Regarding working papers, the author notes that OpenAlex indexes Repec. However, it also indexes many other working paper series in economics (NBER, MRPA, etc.)—which could be specified from the outset for the uninformed reader.

OpenAlex sometimes records full-text content in the abstracts or the first sections of an article instead of the abstract. Consequently, Figure 1 is potentially biased. These issues are often specific to a journal or publisher. How did the author address them?

The author writes that “the five smaller sources add non-English, institutional, and pedagogical coverage that OpenAlex alone does not provide.” However, OpenAlex does include non-English sources. The wording may mislead the reader.

Since the paper outlines the construction of the corpus in general terms but does not focus on a specific research question, I find it extremely difficult to assess the potential of this dataset for conducting research on the history of economic thought regarding “the contested concept of climate finance.” It is clear that this dataset constitutes a good starting point, but it will still require a tremendous amount of cleaning work before it can be credibly used to answer a specific research question.

The organization of the Climate_Finance_Corpus — with a single column containing the main information and an unstructured set of data in the subsequent columns — makes manual check of the corpus rather unpleasant for someone who wishes to explore it manually for potential use.
